title: Designing for a New Use Case
concept: A worked walkthrough choosing the right combination of techniques from this phase for a fresh product requirement.

exercises.prompt: Which system architecture combines embeddings, vector search, and prompting to answer questions using external documents?
exercises.explain: In the prior lesson, we reviewed how retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) brings external knowledge directly into the LLM's prompt window using embeddings and search.

cards.title: From Blank Canvas to Architecture
cards.body: Now that you know all the pieces of the LLM application stack, how do you actually decide which ones to use when a client hands you a brand-new project description? Let's learn how to architect on demand.

cards.title: Use Case Design
cards.body: Designing for a new use case means mapping real-world user needs to specific LLM architectures. Instead of blindly applying every technique, you evaluate tradeoffs like latency, accuracy, and cost to pick the minimum viable stack.

cards.title: The Blueprint and the Toolbox
cards.body: Think of a master builder with a giant toolbox. You don't dump every tool—hammer, saw, and cement mixer—into every small job. Instead, you inspect the blueprint (the user's goal) and select only the exact tools needed for that specific house.

cards.title: Tailoring the Tech Stack
cards.body: If you are building an offline customer support bot for a small company, you might choose local semantic search over a private database, rerank the top 3 hits, and feed them into a small, fast model to generate a friendly response.

cards.title: The 'More is Better' Fallacy
cards.body: Many developers think they must use every tool—like vector databases, agentic loops, and rerankers—for every project. In reality, adding unnecessary layers increases latency, cost, and bugs; simpler designs often perform better.

cards.title: Matching Design to Complexity
cards.body: Compare how we design for a simple task versus a complex, data-heavy task.

left.text: Text translation: Only needs a prompt template and a fast, direct LLM API call.

right.text: Internal knowledge base: Needs chunking, vector search, reranking, and a RAG pipeline.

exercises.prompt: What is the very first step in designing a solution for a new LLM use case?

options.text: Define the application's constraints and user requirements
Choose the largest available LLM parameter size
Set up an vector database on the cloud

exercises.explain: Before writing code, setting up vector databases, or training models, you must understand the exact constraints of the product, such as where the data lives and how fast the responses must be.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Every LLM application requires a vector database, even if it only needs to reformat standard text inputs.
exercises.explain: A simple prompt template is often sufficient if the model already contains the necessary knowledge and does not need external or real-time data.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to describe the core engineering challenge when selecting LLM components.
exercises.sentence: An application engineer must balance tradeoffs between accuracy, speed, and API costs when selecting LLM components.
exercises.explain: System design is all about weighing tradeoffs, such as balancing high accuracy (using a reranker) against low latency (skipping the reranker).

exercises.prompt: Which of the following designs represents an AI-engineered solution for custom document questioning?

options.text: Write a Python script that uses regex matching to search a local text directory for keyword matches.
Create an SQL database containing user profiles and query it using standard inner joins.
Embed user manuals into a vector store, retrieve top chunks matching a query, and pass them to an LLM to generate troubleshooting steps.

exercises.explain: Option B represents a typical template-based or heuristic approach that does not use the LLM stack. Option C is a classic LLM system design solution (Retrieval-Augmented Generation using embeddings and vector search to answer queries based on custom documents).

exercises.prompt: You are designing a secure prescription-checking assistant. It must read a clinic's offline medicine guidelines and automatically update the local clinic database with its findings. Which combination of techniques is most appropriate?

options.text: Chunked local medical files + Vector Retrieval + MCP Tool Calling with JSON Schema
Zero-shot prompting + Web search API + Raw markdown generation
Fine-tuning a model on historical prescriptions + Vector search with no context window limits
Reranking random internet documents + Long-context model without structured outputs

exercises.explain: A local medical prescription app requires absolute factual grounding (RAG with embeddings/chunking to retrieve exact files) and strict, deterministic data output to update the local records (JSON Schema/Tool Calling via MCP). Vector search alone cannot guarantee the structured execution, and prompting alone is prone to hallucination.
